data.label: Move (trajectory action)
data.description: ROS `send_action_goal` to `/xarm6_traj_controller/follow_joint_trajectory` (`control_msgs/action/FollowJointTrajectory`). Commands a single-waypoint trajectory to the proven-reachable joint target [0, -0.3, -0.3, 0, 0.6, 0] (reused from demo 30/32) on the xArm 6 driven through ros2_control fake hardware. Reuses demo 32's exact `config.fields`, `interface_typedefs` and deployment (capacity `xarm_fleet`, requirement `ros.robot_model == xarm6`) VERBATIM — but WITHOUT the feedback channel: here the real-hardware motion runs in parallel with the `twin_stream` branch, which synthesizes the SAME interpolated joint trajectory and streams it as a data channel so the UI can animate a live digital twin on the graph edge. The terminal action RESULT (`error_code`) rides `stdout_tail` ONLY — success is implicit (the backend only succeeds when GoalStatus == 4 SUCCEEDED). The goal is authored as free-form JSON in `config.fields` and passed to rosbridge verbatim. Runs on an `xarm_fleet` runner that satisfies `ros.robot_model == xarm6`; the rosbridge endpoint is runner-local (`EXECUTOR_ROS__WS_URL`).

data.label: Twin joint stream (live UI animation)
data.description: A plain Python AutomatedStep (NO ros requirement) that synthesizes the SAME single-waypoint trajectory the `move` branch commands and streams it as a Data/Out channel `joints` so the UI render registry can animate a live digital twin of the xArm 6 on the graph edge WHILE the real (fake-hardware) arm follows the identical path in the `move` branch. The node linearly interpolates each of the 6 joints from the all-zeros home pose to [0, -0.3, -0.3, 0, 0.6, 0] over ~2.5 s at 30 Hz (~75 frames), and for every frame writes one NDJSON line `{"joint_names":[joint1..joint6],"positions":[6 floats]}` onto the channel via `with open_output('joints') as ch: ch.write(line.encode(), content_type=...)`, pacing at 1/30 s so the tap is perceptibly live. The element content_type is `application/vnd.aithericon.joint-state+x-ndjson` so the presentation dispatch (planLiveRender) routes the stream to the robot-arm twin renderer. The bulk joint stream rides the out-of-band JetStream datastream transport — it NEVER enters the net marking; the net sees only the channel's open + close (2 firings for the whole stream). At the end the node sets `frames` to the number of joint-state frames emitted.

data.label: Twin view (drain joint stream)
data.description: Consumer of the `joints` data channel — the net-side anchor the live 3D URDF twin renders ON (the UI taps the SAME channel out-of-band via `?follow=1`, while this node drains it on the data plane). Reads the NDJSON joint-state frames with the ordinary transport-unaware `for chunk in stream('joints')` API, reframes on newlines and counts complete frames. The robot model is carried in the channel content_type as `;model=xarm6` so the twin renderer knows which URDF (`robot_description` asset, ref_key xarm6) to load even though the PRODUCER is a plain Python node with no ROS requirement. The bulk stream rides the out-of-band JetStream datastream transport — the net sees only open + close.
